text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic myomectomy on a patient with a nine-centimeter intramural myoma located on the fundal and anterior wall of the uterus. Vasopressin is injected above the capsule, causing the uterus to turn marble white. A crescent-shaped incision is made on the uterus using the vibrating jaw of the harmonic scalpel, achieving a good surgical plane. Fluid leakage is observed from the vasopressin solution. The uterus is elevated, and a circumferential incision is made on the anterior wall, supported by the left hand holding the myoma screw, while the right hand makes the incision. The incisions meet, leaving a portion of the serosa attached to the uterus.

The surgeon lifts the uterus using anterior traction with the left hand while employing a harmonic scalpel and suction irrigation to slowly separate the myoma from the uterine tissue. The myoma screw is repeatedly adjusted and applied at the base to optimize traction during the separation process.

The surgeon carefully separates the remaining portion of the myoma from the uterine base using a harmonic device, ensuring gentle dissection to avoid abrupt disruption of the tissue plane and prevent stripping of the endometrium.

The surgeon repositions the myoma screw and uses the harmonic device to separate the remaining attachment of the myoma from the capsule. The sigmoid colon is visible below, requiring careful anterior traction to prevent injury during the separation process.

The surgeon separates the myoma from the uterus and begins suturing the muscle layer using a double-layer intracorporeal continuous technique with a two-third circle needle. A number one Vicryl suture with a 30 mm needle is used to tightly approximate the internal muscle layer, followed by the serous layer.

The surgeon completes the suturing of the muscle layer and begins suturing the serous layer using a needle. The suture is tightened by pulling with the open jaw of the Maryland forceps, ensuring proper traction while passing the needle. The procedure is performed using three laparoscopic ports, without a fourth port for assistance.

The surgeon performs double layer continuous suturing using intracorporeal technique, holding the suture with the right hand and tightening it with the open jaw of the Maryland or needle holder. The tissue is pressed, and the suture is pulled out to ensure tight approximation.

The surgeon cuts and removes the suture while carefully avoiding the iliac vessel. Suction irrigation is performed to remove blood clots, followed by copious lavage. Morselation of the nine-centimeter myoma begins, with visible tissue being gradually morselated. The port used for morselation, measuring fifteen mm, will be closed at the end to prevent hernia formation.

The surgeon performs suction irrigation to remove collected blood from the surgical site following the removal of a nine-centimeter intramural myoma during laparoscopic myomectomy. No drain is used, and the wound closure is completed with intracorporeal suturing.
